title: Part 7
text: which van Gennep had himself pinned down in his critiques. Durkheim saw ‘individualism’ as both an epistemological and a methodological ‘enemy’ to combat, always arguing for the primacy of society. Van Gennep always insisted that individuals make choices and affect social situations in any kind of society, and this even so among the Australian tribes invoked by Durkheim. Van Gennep had made this a very direct critique of Durkheim already in the preface to his 1906 publication on Australian religion: ‘In reality, just as with us, in the Australian tribes it is the individual who invents and proposes modifications’ (van Gennep, here as quoted in Zumwalt 1982: 5). And this is something that anthropologists know quite well from their fieldworks: people, real human beings, even in the smallest and least differentiated settings, can have indeed very strong and distinct personalities. If van Gennep’s critique of Durkheim on this point had been given more resonance, we could have avoided a whole century of social theoretical attempts to posit ‘primitive society’ as an entity without individuality and therefore as an ‘opposite’ to modern human beings. In the first chapter of EFRL, Durkheim pushed forward his argument by a series of dichotomies: all religious beliefs can be placed in two categories, namely the profane and the sacred. But as we know, at the end of the day, the realm of the sacred is for Durkheim nothing but an expression of the profane in need of an ‘object’ to worship. Van Gennep likewise singled out the sacred/profane distinction as important, but as he makes clear in the third sentence of the book, the very stark dichotomy between sacred and profane is indeed a product of post-Renaissance modernity (1960: 1), and not at all present in the same way in ‘semi-civilized’ Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 59 peoples, where the sacred is part of every act and thought (ibid.: 3). Van Gennep proceeded to ground the similarities in ceremonies in the very fact of transition. Transitions from group to group or from one social situation to the next are a ‘fact of existence’ (ibid.). ‘The universe itself is governed by a periodicity which has repercussions on human life, with stages and transitions, movements forward, and periods of relative inactivity’, said van Gennep (ibid.). Much more can be made of the comparison between Durkheim and van Gennep, but for the purposes of our current argument we can stop here: Durkheim established a framework of analysis positing ritual as a timeless consolidation of society, whereas van Gennep had proposed a more open-ended framework of analysis focusing on patterns, and positing transition as the central ‘fact of life’. The point of departure for Van Gennep’s approach was constituted by real human experiences, living facts, and moments of transition, in contrast to Durkheim’s social facts, which became ‘facts’ exactly to the extent that they were external to the individual. Durkheim, said van Gennep, was pressing ethnographic data into a prefabricated and alien theoretical framework. It is a kind of phlogiston alchemy legitimized by an apparently objective scientific procedure by which things that do not fit or belong together are simply forced to fit in a ‘string of dogmatic formulas constructed without regard to the facts’ (as van Gennep would restate it in his 1920 publication on totemism; as quoted in Belmont 1979: 29). These two very different points of departure have very different potential theoretical ramifications. A recovery of van Gennep’s perspective is therefore also of relevance to contemporary theorizing. Arnold van Gennep and Marcel Mauss: So Close and yet so Far Away But how does Marcel Mauss fit in to this whole discussion? A fuller answer to this question lies outside the limits of our discussion, but a few observations must be made as they concern the general reception (and dismissal) of Arnold van Gennep’s work among both anthropologists and sociologists. Moreover, they also concern something that goes against established wisdom, but that has already been hinted at: that Marcel Mauss, despite everything, on many accounts moved sociology closer to the viewpoint of Arnold van Gennep than has so far been recognized. Van Gennep’s relationship to Durkheim and his students was tight and complex, before it suffered a final split. As we saw in Chapter 1, van Gennep had studied sciences religieuses under Léon Marillier with Marcel Mauss. Van Gennep was clearly a promising scholar, someone potentially very close to the Durkheimians. In Rites of Passage, as in other works in that period, van Gennep constructively built on the writings by Mauss, Henri Hubert and Robert Hertz. Mauss certainly knew all of van Gennep’s work. He wrote a slightly critical review of Tabou et Totémisme, but van Gennep reacted very positively to the critique, and thanked Mauss for having signalled how his interpretation could be improved (Zumwalt 1982: 4). Liminality and the Modern 60 More importantly for the present argument, Mauss wrote a short and highly critical review of Rites de Passage in L’année Sociologique (Mauss, 1910; see also Belmont’s discussion, 1979: 62–3). This review is often used as testimony to the fact that Mauss and the Durkheimians did not consider van Gennep’s work important or worthy of much consideration, and allegedly demonstrates that it was van Gennep’s own academic shortcomings and lack of theoretical rigour that prevented his entry into French academia – much more so than any conspiracy against him. Mauss’ critique therefore merits our attention. Mauss’ Review of Rites of Passage Mauss accuses van Gennep on three accounts. First, Mauss says that van Gennep sees rites of passage everywhere, and that he forces this ‘law’ of ritual passages into a principle that governs all religious representations, underpinning the very structure of thought and philosophy, ‘from the Greeks to Nietzsche’. Second, (and here the preceding argument is turned on its head), Mauss says that in a sense van Gennep is of course right about what he is saying (people use rites of passage to demarcate transitions), only that, unfortunately for van Gennep, this was already well known to everyone. Mauss quite simply says that the book is built on a truism, offering nothing new. Third, Mauss accuses the book of a generic defect (which the Durkheimians often pointed out, especially with reference to the British school), namely that of presenting a myriad of ethnographic and historical facts instead of bringing analysis to bear on a circumscribed and thoroughly studied case (Mauss actually comes close to ridiculing the book as ‘une randonnée à travers l’histoire et l’ethnographie’, i.e. a loose narrative jumping randomly around in ethnographic and historical facts). These are without much doubt the least convincing pages that Marcel Mauss ever put his name to. They are also close to embarrassing, and for some quite evident reasons, pertaining to each of the three points of the critique. First, van Gennep is explicitly not proposing any ‘law’. ‘Law’ is not a word that van Gennep even uses in Rites of Passage – whereas it was indeed typical of Durkheim and his followers. 10 The word that van Gennep does use is that of ‘schema’, which quite evidently must be related to shared forms and ‘rhythms’, to patterns that are indeed comparable at the general level, but that do not translate easily into law-like statements concerning ‘causes’ or ‘functions’ of rites. Mauss simply misreads and distorts the entire argument by forcing van Gennep’s analytical effort into his own Durkheimian mould of what ‘theorizing’ may mean – actually here exposing the inherent problem with Durkheimian neo-Kantianism, 10 One year later, in one of his follow-up books, La Formation des légendes, van Gennep actually replaces the earlier employed term ‘schema’ with ‘law’, but openly expresses his own regret at ‘having to use’ this highly problematic term, uncritically borrowed from the natural sciences. Van Gennep justifies his choice, and not without irony, by referring to the fact that ‘some other ethnographers and folklorists have not felt the same scruple’ (in Belmont 1979: 45). Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 61 more so than managing to point out van Gennep’s ‘shortcomings’. Still on this first point of critique, that van Gennep ‘sees rites of passage everywhere’ and extends that ‘law’ beyond the analytically meaningful, it is in fact quite striking what Mauss is actually blaming van Gennep for: van Gennep indicates no aim whatsoever to ground a ‘theory of thought and knowledge’ from the structure of rites of passage. He quotes Nietzsche once (shame on you!), but this is simply with reference to Nietzsche’s notion of the eternal recurrence which seems to resonate well with beliefs among many peoples (he cites the Lushae) who see life itself as circular. 11 It is true that van Gennep’s approach does open up the possibility of an entirely different ‘foundation’ of society, and arguably even of conceptual thought: namely in human experiences of, and responses to, liminality. Rather than following Durkheimian dogmatism, such a theory of foundation would have had to move completely outside a (neo-)Kantian framework, and not simply solve the problem as posed from within it. To some extent, it was this idea that Victor Turner became engaged with later in his career when he encountered the work of Dilthey: the relationship between thought and experience in liminal moments. We shall have a chance to return to this discussion. But van Gennep? Van Gennep had made no such claim about presenting a theory of knowledge. So where does this come from? Well, it probably comes from Durkheim. In his Introduction to EFRL, Durkheim famously made the hubristic claim to have solved the philosophical problem of knowledge. By grounding Kant’s a priori in social facts, in social divisions and categories, Durkheim claimed to have established not only the origin of society and religion, but equally the explanation of how we can know things at all. Durkheim was working on that theory in that very period. So instead of detecting a potential point from which to revise their own positions concerning cognition and thought, what Mauss does is to inject van Gennep’s book with an ambition it simply does not express, a straw-man which he then duly demolishes. Second, it may well be argued that in one sense Rites of Passage is a truism. However, and as Mauss again fails to recognize, that is exactly what van Gennep himself claims: what he argues is not a deduction or even an analytical extrapolation, and he is expressly not proposing any division or classification which is not already present in the material itself. 12 Most genuine human and scientific discoveries possess this ‘A-ha’ nature: but of course! We see something 11 Van Gennep rarely if ever made analytical ‘leaps’ to more purely philosophical debates, always arguing from the ground of available data. The only philosopher van Gennep mentions in Rites of Passage is in fact Nietzsche, in the second-to-last paragraph of the book. This is where van Gennep compares rectilinear to cyclical patterns, and notes how the circular order from life to death and death to life (the sequential order of the book itself) acquired a ‘psychological significance’ in Nietzsche’s theory of eternal recurrence (1960: 194). 12 It is striking how Mauss even on this point will later start to emulate van Gennep. In his crucial 1927 publication, where Mauss proposes a new division of sociology and its subject fields, he justifies the proposed division as follows: ‘The division is clear and distinct. It does not divide anything which is not perfectly divided in reality’ (Mauss 2005: 60). Liminality and the Modern 62 which was there, but which we previously just could not see – obvious at it was, yet not fully visible; something which we just could not pin down with precise words. The related point to stress is that this was indeed, at the very same time, fundamentally new. No-one had said it before, and no-one has ever since said it so clearly and so convincingly. Mauss was quite plainly wrong about his assessment. Van Gennep’s classificatory effort has stood the test of time. It is not something we can say about a lot of books written in that period. What van Gennep said was not only new, it was genuinely ground-breaking and indeed foundational. One of the reasons why this is so has exactly to do with the foregrounding of the central, intermediate stage where transition and transformation occur, the liminal aspect that was wholly missing in the approaches offered by the Durkhemians. Third, it is true that van Gennep ‘gallops’ across space and time in his collection of ethnographic data. However, the data itself is sound enough – Mauss has nothing substantial to put his finger on. So what is the problem? Is the data unorganized, as Mauss seems to indicate? The answer is no. The material is extremely convincingly argued and elegantly presented, following through with the tripartite structure, pushing the narrative across the various life-stages of an individual, and for each type of ritual passage drawing into the discussion an otherwise complex ethnographic data set. There were plenty of books written in the period (and since!) that left the reader drowning in data without any clear picture or conceptual frame to hold on to. Van Gennep’s book does not belong there; it is a beacon of light, a fresh breath of analytical clarity and ethnographic rigour in the emerging life sciences. If ever there was ‘grounded theory’, then this was it. 13 And here a spontaneous observation presents itself: if there is one book that resembles Rites of Passage, in terms of structure, procedure, and mode of theorizing, zooming in and out between history, ethnography, etymology and comparative theory, and with a keen perception of another universal tripartite formal structure emerging from the ethnography, then it is another small classic written some years later, and by Marcel Mauss himself: The Gift. It was certainly not a review that did justice to the book, and even the tone of the review leaves the reader with a feeling that Mauss was not sincere. Did the review express Mauss’ own reading, or did he feel forced into defending the Durkheimian position? Or had he in fact been pressed into doing so? L’année Sociologique was Durkheim’s journal; he decided on everything that was to be published there, often down to detail. Mauss’ short and harsh review might simply have been commissioned (co-written?) by Durkheim as pay-back for the earlier critiques launched by van Gennep against Durkheim. Instead of discussing those critiques in merit, actually having to confront them, one simply waits until 13 What Mauss exercises here, is a sheer repetition of a critique that the Durkheimians always launched against members of the British school, including Frazer. Van Gennep was in many ways closer to the British school of anthropology, not least with regard to its empirical foundations, but what Mauss here fails to appreciate is the fact that van Gennep did not simply take over the comparative methodology of the British school. Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 63 van Gennep publishes his next book, and then destroys it. If this is the strategy that Durkheim followed, it can never be proven. Durkheim wrote very few letters to Mauss in that period, and for the simple reason that they now both lived in Paris. From 1907 to 1911, besides his flat on Rue de Cluny, Mauss occupied a room on Rue Saint-Jacques right above Durkheim’s apartment, which he almost certainly used for work (Besnard and Fournier 1998: 349). As we know from the general context of their relationship, Mauss very much lived and worked under Durkheim’s law-like dictates in that period. It is at least safe to say that Mauss was not free to write what he thought about someone who had exposed Durkheim so openly in his earlier writings. And the fact that the relationship between Durkheim and Mauss was becoming extremely strenuous and conflict-ridden in those very years (when World War I broke out, Mauss experienced life in the army as a genuine liberation!) may not be unrelated to what was going on here. Leaving speculation aside, there are however substantial reasons to assume that Mauss eventually took up van Gennep’s work in more than one way, albeit silently so, and without ever paying full credit to his sources of inspiration. Van Gennep’s and Mauss’ interests overlapped in a myriad of ways, and there is one legacy which is worth stressing again here, as it concerns gift-giving, no doubt Mauss’ most influential and important legacy. Van Gennep paid great attention to systems of exchange, and may here have inspired both Mauss and Lévi-Strauss more than has so far been recognized, and more than both Mauss and Lévi-Strauss themselves ever conceded. Chapter 3 14 in The Rites of Passage is almost entirely dedicated to ritualistic exchange of words, gestures, services, goods, slaves, and wives: precisely the aspects of exchange that Mauss systematically took up in his famous essay The Gift, and which Lévi-Strauss used as the platform for his exchange theory. Mauss does not cite or even mention van Gennep’s Rites of Passage in The Gift. And yet, the circulation of gifts is a central aspect of ritualization for van Gennep. Certain ‘gifts’ are obligatorily given, said van Gennep: the circulation of goods and objects serves to create continuous social bonds (1960: 31). Gift giving is the ‘confirmation of a bond’, and ‘to accept a gift is to be bound to the giver’ (ibid.: 29). Van Gennep then discusses, among others, the potlatch as one peculiar example, as he discusses gift-giving practices among warrior groups as a peace- instituting act; van Gennep further notes how incorporation rites are often tied to military, sexual and political rights (ibid.: 35). Van Gennep had been a student of Mauss. But Mauss was also a student of van Gennep. These overlaps and convergences are not surprising: they go back to the joint academic formation of Mauss and van Gennep, and what can be reconstructed as an indeed very similar approach, if not ‘temper’. Van Gennep once remarked 14 This is the conceptual discussion entitled Individuals and Groups which precedes the six following chapters that each deal with a cross-cultural examination of ritual passages as they pertain to the life cycle, from pregnancy to death. In other words, van Gennep posits gift-giving as a foundational principle at play in ritual phenomena, tying individuals and groups together. Liminality and the Modern 64 upon this similarity himself. In a 1905 review of a journal issue of L’Année Sociologique, van Gennep asks himself with genuine surprise: why it is that his own work, though so essentially similar to Mauss’s, is so openly criticized in L’Année Sociologique, while Mauss’s was obviously accepted: ‘Mauss posed some rules of method which are like those which I presented here (one nearly reproached me for them) ... which I applied in Tabou et Totémisme à Madagascar, formulated with more precision in my Notes sur le Totémisme ...’ (van Gennep [1905] as quoted in Zumwalt 1982: 4). It must also be stressed that neither Mauss’ critique of Rites of Passage, nor van Gennep’s devastating critique of EFRL led to a final rupture between the two thinkers. In fact, in 1913, Mauss positively reviewed van Gennep’s Algerian ethnography (Zumwalt 1982: 4). Mauss praised van Gennep for the incredible ethnographic effort and the analytical precision found in the work. These were exactly what van Gennep had pointed out as fatal weaknesses in Durkheim’s EFRL, published within weeks. In fact, we know with certainty that while on the surface the split between van Gennep and the Durkheimians had become irrevocable, the bridges between van Gennep and Mauss were never totally burnt. 15 During World War I, when van Gennep realized that he would sooner or later leave Switzerland, he could find only one person to contact in order to help him back into French academia: Marcel Mauss. The exchange that followed provides one little glimpse into Durkheim’s role in keeping van Gennep out of French academia. Arnold van Gennep’s Fatal ‘Encounter’ with Durkheim via Mauss in 1915 In 1915 van Gennep attempted to re-enter French academia, and he did so initially via Mauss. While we do not have access to the letter(s) which van Gennep initially directed to Mauss, the main point relating to the content can be inferred from the letters Durkheim later wrote to Mauss, and which we fortunately do have access to. In this extremely delicate situation, in the middle of World War I, van Gennep had asked for Mauss’ support, and he quite evidently also asked Mauss if it would be proper of him to contact Durkheim directly. Durkheim had by then established an enormous power position concerning employment within the social sciences; it would be hard to get anywhere without at least his consent. And Mauss apparently told van Gennep to go ahead, and that he had his recommendations. There is of course also another hypothetical scenario (and one does not exclude the other, quite the contrary): that Mauss and van Gennep had talked very openly 15 As late as the 1930s, when Mauss had become Chair at the Collège, van Gennep was still fighting to secure some kind of stable income for his old age. Van Gennep would seek Mauss’ support for the creation of a Chair in French and comparative folklore at the Collège or solicit his help towards a position as director of studies in religion or folklore (Fournier 2006: 300). It was again to no avail, but the fact that he even tried again shows that their relationship was more complex than it may seem at a first glance. Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 65 about the employment situation in France as they met during the conference in Neuchâtel in 1914. Mauss had come to the conference on van Gennep’s invitation. As old colleagues it is likely that they did talk together. This is all the more plausible, given the timing and the general context: the declared aim of the whole conference was to lay out the framework for the establishment of the ethnographic and social sciences, including not only epistemological and methodological issues, but indeed also the content and institutional form that new university degrees in the social sciences should take. Mauss was working on the very same issues in France; and Mauss and van Gennep converged in essential ways concerning their discipline, not least its methodology. The conference took place just weeks before the outbreak of World War I. The war was in the air, and van Gennep may have had a presentiment that his permanence in Switzerland might be in jeopardy. Or maybe he quite simply missed France, and hoped to come back. We know that Van Gennep, after having sought the consent of Mauss, made contact with Durkheim during the spring of 1915; he was only formally expelled from Switzerland during the fall of that year. There can be little doubt that Van Gennep still – after all – nurtured ambitions to eventually enter French academia. His applications to the Collége had come to nothing, but his ambitions had not evaporated. With a permanent position and at a distance from French academic power struggles, he may now have felt more comfortable attempting to re-launch himself, despite the fact that he and Mauss had, just a few years before, been competing for the same jobs in Paris. And there is safe ground to assume that van Gennep, despite everything, continued to think highly of Mauss. Van Gennep may have felt that he by now represented even less of a threat to Durkheim and his followers, whose power and influence had by then become very hegemonic. If this is so, he was wrong. Durkheim wrote two letters to Mauss with van Gennep as a topic, the first in April–May 1915 (precise date not legible), the second on 1 June 1915 (Durkheim 1998: 455–7; 461–2). The wording in the first letter deserves to be quoted in full: Received a letter from Van Gennep who presents his candidature for a maitrise de conference in the province. He authorizes himself with advice from you: you supposedly have said to him: ‘it is a shame that it is Switzerland [and not France] that is profiting from his scientific activity’. He asks me that I support him. I fail to apprehend on which basis he may nurture such hopes; for he has no degrees, if I am not wrong. Nonetheless, after what he has written about me, I cannot help to say what I think about his candidature, which struck me as being over the top [dénoter du toupet 16 ]. (Durkheim 1998: 456; my translation) 16 The expression ‘dénoter du toupet’ is not easily translatable into English. In the context, it probably would be more correct to translate in the direction of: ‘he has got some courage!’, or ‘how dare he even try to apply!’. The sense is that Durkheim perceives van Gennep’s application as an insult, an outright act of rudeness. Liminality and the Modern 66 It is entirely likely that this is precisely what Mauss sincerely thought: that it was utterly meaningless that one of France’s leading ethnographers and social scientists was wasting his time in Switzerland, right at the moment when things were starting to take shape in France. Despite everything, Mauss would almost certainly have liked to see his former, most brilliant and productive graduate student back in the fold. And it is sometimes tempting to imagine what would have happened, had Mauss and van Gennep taken up their collaboration from the first years of the century – for there can be no doubt whatsoever that it would have resulted a decisive collaboration for the formation of the social sciences in France. But counterfactual history remains what it is: something that never happened. As we saw above, Durkheim never once responded to van Gennep’s detailed critiques of his work. This is the primary reason why so many social scientists since then have managed to completely bypass van Gennep’s real importance during those formative years – they simply have not read his work, cataloguing him as a folklorist without theoretical scope. Durkheim does not discuss him either (as opposed to Tarde who is at least singled out as a conversation partner), so why bother? Unsurprisingly, what we can infer from these lines of the letter is that Durkheim had indeed read van Gennep’s critique, and that it still disturbed him – and also that he continued to hold this bitterly against van Gennep, even as his own career and position were more than secured. There was going to be no forgiveness. In the letter Durkheim also expresses doubts about van Gennep’s titles – and this is another myth that has circulated ever since in French academic circles: that the Durkheimians could not take van Gennep seriously because he had no real titles, that he was an autodidact, a strange fellow with some funny ideas, but ultimately someone from outside the system. Sure enough, van Gennep was not yet Docteur; that title was finally bestowed on him in 1922. However, he did hold two university titles already, and he had by then published quite extensively – just possibly more than any other social scientist of his entire generation. If titles had been the issue, a great many of the persons who were indeed hired to university positions in France during those years (and often under Durkheim’s very direct influence) would have been disqualified. Titles mattered when they mattered. And here of course lies the double bind in the message from Durkheim to Mauss: for if there was one person who had a real problem with his titles and formal credentials, it wasn’t van Gennep. It was Marcel Mauss. Mauss had still not even finished his doctoral thesis. And he never did. And formally speaking, he had still not published a proper book. Arnold van Gennep had by then twelve books on his record. And he was also running his own journal. Mauss’ letters to Durkheim were destroyed by the Nazis during World War II, so we do not know how he responded to Durkheim concerning the thorny issue of van Gennep. Perhaps he did not respond at all, for when Durkheim takes up the issue again – the day after van Gennep’s candidature has been evaluated – it is Durkheim that reminds Mauss of the issue, and brings him up-to-date with the situation (ibid.: 462): Arnold van Gennep and his Contemporaries 67 You no doubt received my letter in which I told you that Van Gennep had, on your advice, he claims, presented his candidature. The question came up yesterday at the consultative committee. I argued his case. But the lack of university titles seemed decisive. It was further discussed whether he had been naturalised French. Do you know anything about this subject? His father was a Würtembergian army officer. [...] I could not accept the expression, ‘first rank works’ used by Jullian 17 to assess his books. (italics as original, my translation) This message again is layered with meaning. With this letter Durkheim in reality sends Mauss two contradictory messages that smack of yet another double bind. First, Durkheim is quite evidently upset and ‘surprised’ that van Gennep had presented himself to him with Mauss’s recommendation (the italics contain a thinly veiled accusation that Mauss had even dared to recommend van Gennep). Second, Durkheim claims that he has ‘supported’ his candidature, so in principle there is nothing Mauss, or anyone else, can hold against him – van Gennep simply was not qualified enough, as it turned out. From Durkheim’s summary, it seems that three aspects were discussed in the committee which turned out decisive: van Gennep’s titles, his nationality, and the quality of his work. And it is more than evident from the context in what direction Durkheim had actually argued during the deliberation: that his titles were insufficient, that his nationality was a big question mark, 18 and – most importantly – that Durkheim, in contrast to
